ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a broccoli food product entry; no dedicated stir-fried broccoli dish entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated stir-fried broccoli with aromatics entry. Cooked broccoli code A00LC used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for stir-fried broccoli dish. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried broccoli weighs approximately 250 g, typical of a side vegetable dish served at a Singapore hawker centre or Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried broccoli — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient composition data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for stir-fried broccoli (cr_id F14080039). Micronutrients calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170379, Broccoli, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt) and adjusted for stir-frying preparation. Potassium estimated from USDA FoodData Central broccoli cooked data (~166 mg/100 g after cooking losses). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central vegetable oil and broccoli composite. Water content estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked broccoli (~89 g/100 g).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Vegetables — Broccoli analogous estimate based on non-starchy cruciferous vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI test for stir-fried broccoli with aromatics exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Broccoli and other non-starchy cruciferous vegetables are consistently reported with GI values in the range of 10–25 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). A GI of 15 is used as a defensible estimate for this preparation. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 7.1) / 100 = 1.07, rounded to 1.1. Carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 2.84 × 2.5 = 7.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 342 mg/100 g — borderline elevated for a vegetable dish due to oyster sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to bring sodium below 200 mg/100 g; the natural potassium (~166 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (2.56 g/100 g) in broccoli support DASH dietary principles and partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.84 g/100 g), low GI (~15), high dietary fibre (2.56 g/100 g), minimal sugar (0.96 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried broccoli is an excellent choice for type 2 diabetes management due to its negligible glycaemic impact (GI ~15, GL ~1.1 per 250 g serving), high fibre content slowing glucose absorption, and sulforaphane compounds that may improve insulin sensitivity; patients should minimise added sugar in the sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g), zero trans fat, high vitamin C (40 mg/100 g), and anti-inflammatory sulforaphane from broccoli
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried broccoli is cardioprotective due to very low saturated fat, absence of trans fat, and broccoli's sulforaphane and vitamin C content which reduce oxidative stress and vascular inflammation; sodium should be reduced by limiting oyster and soy sauce for patients with concurrent hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sulforaphane and vitamin C (40 mg/100 g) provide anti-inflammatory and antioxidant benefit; very low saturated fat and zero trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Broccoli is one of the most beneficial vegetables for rheumatoid arthritis due to sulforaphane's anti-inflammatory properties; patients should reduce oyster and soy sauce to lower sodium and minimise RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried broccoli (炒西兰花, Brokoli goreng) is a popular vegetable side dish in Singapore and across Chinese-influenced Southeast Asian cuisines. Fresh broccoli florets are wok-fried over high heat with aromatics such as garlic and ginger, then seasoned with oyster sauce and soy sauce, producing a dish that is tender-crisp in texture with a savoury, slightly umami flavour. The high-heat wok technique preserves much of the broccoli's natural colour, vitamin C content (40 mg/100 g), and dietary fibre (2.56 g/100 g). Per 100 g, the dish provides 72 kcal, 2.88 g protein, 5.16 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil), and only 2.84 g carbohydrate with a glycaemic index of approximately 15, making it one of the most diabetes-friendly vegetable preparations available. Sodium is 342 mg/100 g due to oyster and soy sauce, which is the primary dietary caution for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. Broccoli's sulforaphane content provides anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is vegetarian-suitable (with oyster sauce substitution for vegan), halal, and dairy-free. A standard serving is approximately 250 g (1 plate), delivering approximately 180 kcal and 7.1 g carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of only 1.1.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried broccoli?
Is stir-fried broccoli good for diabetes?
Is stir-fried broccoli high in sodium?
What is the GI of broccoli?
Can I eat stir-fried broccoli if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080039, confidence 0.95). Micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked broccoli data. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous non-starchy cruciferous vegetable data with documented basis. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Monounsaturated fat (2.1 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (1.5 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked broccoli and typical vegetable oil used in stir-frying; not directly reported in HPB source. Potassium (166 mg/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked broccoli (FDC ID 170379). Water content (89 g/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked broccoli. Vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient data for this specific stir-fried preparation is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level; USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170379) was consulted but dish-level composite data for these nutrients was not available with sufficient confidence for this preparation. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB source data. Calcium (32 mg/100 g), iron (0.6 mg/100 g), cholesterol (2 mg/100 g), saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g), and vitamin C (40 mg/100 g) were originally attributed to grabfood in the input meta; these values have been cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central cooked broccoli and are considered plausible and retained. The dish is vegetarian but not strictly vegan due to oyster sauce; a vegan variant using mushroom sauce is common. Sodium of 342 mg/100 g reflects typical hawker-style preparation with oyster and soy sauce.
